source.subject: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5
source.body:
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 4, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5

Hi all —

We’re ready for cross-functional review on the refreshed Support escalation playbook (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). Goal is to publish the Confluence pages early next week and start using the new severity + SLA language immediately for new escalations.

Docs to review:
- Escalation Playbook Refresh v0.9 (attached)
- Sign-off checklist (attached)
- Working notes (Drive): shared_drives/customer-success/escalation-playbook-refresh-working-notes

Requested sign-off by: Thu 2/5 EOD PT

What I need from each group:
1) SRE (Sean/Rafael)
   - Confirm paging thresholds + escalation triggers are consistent with on-call + SLO alerting
   - Confirm Slack bridge / #incidents handoff rules
   - Confirm we are not creating “backdoor paging” paths

2) Platform (Priya)
   - Confirm ownership boundaries + primary/backup contacts for: SSO, Audit Logs, Quota/Rate Limits, Routing/Fallback, Private installer
   - Confirm after-hours escalation expectations for Private/Dedicated

3) CS leadership (Jasmine/Rachel)
   - Confirm SLA language aligns with tiering + contract expectations
   - Confirm update cadence wording (especially enterprise) and credits/escalation triggers

Sign-off checklist (what I’m looking for in replies):
- [ ] Severity rubric: P0–P4 mapping to Sev + examples make sense
- [ ] Tiered SLAs: first response + update cadence + escalation thresholds are correct
- [ ] Escalation matrix: who/when/how to page is correct + backup coverage
- [ ] Slack bridge convention: naming + required context + who owns customer updates
- [ ] Jira guidance: required fields + SLA timers + linking conventions are workable
- [ ] Exec escalation: rules + summary template are accurate

How to respond:
- Please reply-all with either “Approved” or “Blocker” + the specific section/page. If “Blocker,” include the proposed replacement wording.

Thanks — this will unblock the known-issues sprint workflows + reduce the ping-pong during incidents.

Dev Patel
Support Ops Lead
Redwood Inference

Attachments:
- Escalation_Playbook_Refresh_v0.9.pdf
- Signoff_Checklist_Escalation_Playbook_v1.xlsx


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 4, 2026 at 11:26 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5

Reviewed. Mostly good — one blocker + a couple nits.

BLOCKER:
- SLA table currently implies we page SRE for “P1 enterprise” by default even if impact is isolated to a single tenant with an available workaround.
  - Proposed wording change: make paging trigger “customer-impacting outage/degradation meeting Sev1+ thresholds” rather than “customer tier.” Tier should influence update cadence + comms, not paging.
  - Specifically: in Escalation Triggers, replace “Enterprise P1 => page SRE” with “Page SRE when: (a) elevated error rate/latency affecting multiple customers or a region, OR (b) one customer but no workaround and business-critical workflow blocked.”

Nits:
- Please add “request IDs + region + model + approximate start time + recent deploy (if known)” as required fields in the page template. That’s the minimum for on-call.
- Bridge naming: can we standardize on “sup-<ticket>-<region>-<shortdesc>” so it’s searchable and tied to Jira?

If you incorporate the blocker wording, I’m good to approve.

Sean
SRE


---

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 4, 2026 at 1:04 PM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5

Thanks Dev.

Platform ownership looks correct with two changes:
- Quota/Rate Limits: primary = Control Plane on-call (Platform), backup = SRE if it’s correlated with broader degradation. Please avoid directing Support to ping individual engineers.
- Audit Logs: primary = Platform (data pipeline), but escalation path should include Security/Compliance only if customer is requesting formal attestation / evidence (otherwise it’s a Platform+SRE operational issue).

Other notes:
- Private installer issues: if a customer is mid-upgrade and stuck, we should trigger a Platform page once “rollback not possible” is confirmed (your checklist mentions this; just make it a bold callout).

Not a blocker. If you incorporate the ownership text above, I’m approved.

Priya Natarajan
VP Engineering, Platform


---

From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 9:18 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5

Reviewed from CS + enterprise SLA perspective.

Edits requested (not blockers):
- “Every 30 minutes” updates for enterprise P0: please clarify that cadence is during active mitigation window; if we’re “Waiting on Customer” we should explicitly state we will resume updates when new info is available, but still keep a time-based check-in (e.g., every 2 hours) to avoid silence.
- Credits guidance: please add “credits decisions require CS leadership approval” (Jasmine or me) and should not be promised in the initial acknowledgement.

Otherwise approved.

Rachel Kim
Enterprise Customer Success


---

From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 9:44 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5

Approved with Rachel’s wording change on update cadence + credits.

One additional request: in the comms templates, add a single sentence that sets expectations on what we can share (“we’ll share mitigation steps and status; we may not be able to share full root cause immediately”).

Thanks for pushing this through.

Jasmine Liu
VP Customer Success & Support


---

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 11:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation Playbook refresh — cross-functional review + sign-off by Thu 2/5

Thanks all — incorporated changes and closing out sign-off.

Updates made (v1.0 draft in Confluence now):
- Paging triggers updated per Sean: tier influences comms cadence; paging based on Sev thresholds / lack of workaround. (Also added required context: request IDs, region, model, start time, recent deploy.)
- Bridge naming standardized to: sup-<SUP#>-<region>-<shortdesc>
- Platform ownership updated per Priya (quota/rate limits + audit logs + private installer bold callout)
- Enterprise comms cadence clarified per Rachel (active mitigation vs Waiting on Customer) + added explicit “credits require CS leadership approval; do not promise”
- Added comms template line per Jasmine re: what we can/can’t share during active incident

Next steps:
- I’m kicking off INT-332/INT-333 updates today so workflows match the playbook (bridge template + Jira required fields/SLA reminders).
- Target publish date remains Mon 2/9 (with internal announcement).

If anyone has final blockers after the edits, please reply by end of day. Otherwise I’ll treat this as final approval.

Dev

source.subject: Dedicated tier: remediation SLA alignment + standard customer comms language
source.body:
From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 2, 2026 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Dedicated tier: remediation SLA alignment + standard customer comms language

Team — following up on the Dedicated PIRs/calls (Zenlytics + a couple others) and PM-912. We need to lock the Dedicated remediation SLA matrix + the *exact* language CS/SRE uses when we communicate “mitigation” vs “fix.”

Asks:
1) Confirm Dedicated tier remediation SLAs (time-to-mitigate, time-to-fix) for Sev0/Sev1/Sev2.
2) Confirm carve-outs (cloud provider, customer-caused/config, force majeure) and how we say that without sounding like we’re dodging.
3) Agree on a standard “first update” and “ongoing update” template Dedicated customers get (email + ticket), keyed off the incident taxonomy.

Proposal (Dedicated):
- Sev0 (customer hard down / sustained high 5xx / can’t serve traffic):
  - Time-to-mitigate: 30 minutes
  - Time-to-fix: 4 hours
- Sev1 (major degradation; elevated latency/partial errors impacting a material portion of traffic):
  - Time-to-mitigate: 60 minutes
  - Time-to-fix: 24 hours
- Sev2 (minor degradation / reduced redundancy / non-critical feature impact):
  - Time-to-mitigate: 4 hours
  - Time-to-fix: 5 business days

Definitions (needs agreement):
- Mitigate = restore service to within SLO *or* establish safe degraded mode (e.g., capacity cap + stable 200s) with explicit customer approval if it changes behavior.
- Fix = root cause addressed and rolled out (or permanent config change applied), with regression guardrails.

If we align today, I’ll have CS roll out the comms template and we’ll point incident-bot/Console views at the same definitions.

Product Lead, Dedicated
Redwood Inference


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 2, 2026 at 10:04 AM PT
Subject: Re: Dedicated tier: remediation SLA alignment + standard customer comms language

Works for me in principle. Two flags from SRE side:

1) “Mitigate” has to be measurable in incident tooling. I’d recommend we state “Mitigate = customer impact materially reduced and trending to normal OR stable workaround in place,” and we tie it to the incident commander calling “mitigated” in incident-bot (timestamped). Otherwise we’ll argue about whether we hit the SLA.

2) Carve-outs: we should be explicit that the timer still runs, but we can mark “SLA paused / not-applicable” with a reason code (CSP outage, customer network/config, force majeure). That decision should be reviewable (postmortem or a follow-up ticket). If the messaging is too legal-y, customers will hate it.

I’m fine with your TTM/TTF numbers as a starting point, but I want Rachel/CS input on what they’ve already implied in renewals.

If helpful, the Confluence matrix currently has:
- Dedicated Sev0 TTM 30m / TTF 4h
- Dedicated Sev1 TTM 60m / TTF 24h
- Dedicated Sev2 TTM 4h / TTF 5bd
So this matches.

Rafael
SRE Program Lead


From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 2, 2026 at 11:21 AM PT
Subject: Re: Dedicated tier: remediation SLA alignment + standard customer comms language

+1 to Rafael’s definition tightness.

From an Enterprise Comms perspective, the numbers are fine *if* we’re very clear that:
- These are remediation targets (ops SLA), not a contractual “service credit” SLA unless explicitly in the customer’s MSA/order form.
- “Fix” may span multiple deploy steps (rollback now, permanent fix later). We should say “we will provide a fix plan + ETA” within the TTF window even if the actual patch rollout is gated by safety.

Language suggestion for carve-outs (plain English):
“We’ll drive mitigation as quickly as possible. If the primary driver is a broader cloud provider outage or an issue in customer-managed networking/configuration, we’ll still coordinate mitigation and keep you updated, but remediation timelines may depend on the upstream fix or required customer action.”

I’m also strongly against using “root cause” in the first customer update. We should stick to taxonomy (“capacity saturation,” “routing/fallback misbehavior,” etc.) until we confirm.

Rachel Kim
Enterprise Comms / SLAs
Redwood Inference


From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 2, 2026 at 1:06 PM PT
Subject: Re: Dedicated tier: remediation SLA alignment + standard customer comms language

CS view: agree with the matrix and the comms guardrails.

What we need is a template that:
- Tells the customer *what they might see* (e.g., 429s, streaming disconnects, latency) and what we’re doing
- Sets update cadence expectations
- Doesn’t over-promise on TTF when mitigation is already in place

Draft “first update” (Dedicated) that we can standardize:

Subject: [Dedicated] Service degradation in <region> affecting <cluster/name>

Hi <Name>,

We’re investigating an active incident impacting your Dedicated deployment. Current impact: <brief symptoms + start time>. Our incident classification is <incident type/subtype>.

What we’re doing now:
- <top 1–2 mitigations in progress>

Next update:
- We will provide an update by <timestamp> (or sooner if status changes).

Targets:
- Our remediation target for this severity is mitigation within <TTM> and a fix within <TTF>. “Mitigation” means reducing customer impact to a stable/acceptable level (or returning to SLO). “Fix” means the underlying issue is addressed and rolled out safely.

If you have a critical business event we should be aware of, reply here and we’ll align on a mitigation that fits.

— Redwood Support

Open question: do we want to include the Targets block in *every* Dedicated incident email, or only Sev0/Sev1?

Dev Patel
Support Escalation Mapping


From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 2, 2026 at 3:18 PM PT
Subject: Re: Dedicated tier: remediation SLA alignment + standard customer comms language

Decision from my side:

1) Approve the Dedicated remediation SLA targets as proposed (Sev0 30m/4h, Sev1 60m/24h, Sev2 4h/5bd).
2) Include the “Targets” block in Sev0 and Sev1 customer updates. For Sev2, include on request or when asked; otherwise keep it shorter.
3) “Mitigated” must be declared in incident-bot by IC with a timestamp. If we pause/override the SLA, it requires a reason code + follow-up review.

Lauren/Rafael: please ensure these definitions match the incident taxonomy + remediation SLA tooling so reporting isn’t a mess.

Sean Gallagher
Exec Sponsor (SRE)
Redwood Inference

FINAL (Dedicated remediation targets):

- Sev1: mitigate 60m / fix 24h
- Sev2: mitigate 4h / fix 5 business days

Carve-outs language (use in email if applicable):
“If the primary driver is a broader cloud provider outage or an issue in customer-managed networking/configuration, we’ll coordinate mitigation and keep you updated, but timelines may depend on upstream restoration or required customer action.”

Redwood Inference

I’ll post the final template in the CS/SRE channel once Rafael confirms the incident-bot fields are wired.

Lauren Bishop

Redwood Inference

messages:
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
To: Alyssa Green <alyssa.green@lumenitas.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 15:02:00 -0800
Subject: POC acceptance checklist & Lumenitas sandbox next steps

Hi Alyssa / Devon,

Thanks again for the time today — great conversation. This thread is a short recap + proposed POC acceptance slate so we can lock the sandbox timeline. High-level we heard you want: reliable summarization for sales notes, sub-200ms p95 latency for short prompts, and predictable cost per user for the first 30 days.

Proposed POC (4-week sandbox) acceptance criteria (please flag anything missing):
- Latency: p95 inference latency <= 180ms for 512-token context (measured cold and warm cache)
- Throughput: support sustained 30 qps with <10% tail amplification (target 95th percentile stability)
- Cost: effective token unit cost <= $0.00095 (benchmarked on the expected traffic profile)
- Quality: human-eval pass rate >= 90% on the seed test set (50 representative sessions) and no >5% regression on key intents
- Reliability: error rate <= 0.5% and automatic fallback to a smaller model on overload
- Operational: API key rotation, request tracing headers, and logs retained for 30 days

Deliverables and owners (suggested):
- Redwood: baseline benchmarking report + runbook (Ben)
- Redwood SRE: sandbox infra config + throttling policy (Monica)
- Lumenitas: canonical seed dataset and admin test accounts (Alyssa / Devon)

Next steps (week zero):
1) Lumenitas to share seed dataset and any evaluation scripts by EOD Friday (1/23)
2) Redwood shares sandbox runbook + cost projection by Tuesday (1/21)
3) Schedule smoke test and handoff call for week of 1/26

Attachments: I attached a one-pager spreadsheet (Lumenitas_POC_success_criteria.xlsx) with the proposed metrics and a draft scope.

If that looks good we'll schedule a technical kickoff with Ben + Monica.

Thanks,
Rafael

--
Rafael Mendes
Director, GTM Engineering
Redwood Inference

To: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>

Date: Tue, 20 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800

Thanks for the recap Rafael. A few technical clarifications from my side that will affect the benchmarks and runbook:
- Evaluation workload: please include at least 2 representative peak windows (10–15 min each) with expected QPS profile; we need a realistic traffic trace to calibrate batching.
- Tokenization: confirm which tokenizer you use for the sales notes (we saw 1.1–1.6x difference when glue tokens are present).
- Cold vs Warm: we'll run both cold-start and warm-cache scenarios. Cold-start should be measured after a 30-minute idle window.

Attached: redwood-sandbox-runbook.md (technical checklist + smoke tests) and a small script to collect latency histograms during the smoke test.

Suggested immediate action items for Lumenitas: share a 24-hour sample traffic trace (anonymized) and the tokenizer config. Once we have that Ben will produce the benchmark matrix you can sign off on.

-ben

--
Ben Carter

Redwood Inference

From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Alyssa Green <alyssa.green@lumenitas.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 21 Jan 2026 11:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: POC acceptance checklist & Lumenitas sandbox next steps

Thanks Rafael / Ben — adding ops/SLAs and a timeline.

Operational runbook highlights and timeline (owner: Monica Patel):
- Week 0 (1/19–1/23): dataset + trace ingestion, API key exchange, minimal infra provisioning.
- Week 1 (1/26–1/30): baseline benchmarking (cold/warm), smoke tests, and first round of perf tuning.
- Week 2 (2/2–2/6): stability run (48–72h continuous at expected load), monitoring dashboards live, and cost projection review.
- Week 3 (2/9–2/13): final validation with Lumenitas test sessions and go/no-go decision.

Operational checks:
- Trace collection enabled + sample dashboards (Grafana link will be shared after infra is up)
- Alerting thresholds: latency p95 > 200ms, error rate > 0.5%
- Fallback behavior validated by chaos test (simulate a GPU OOM and confirm fallback within 2s)

Owners & points of contact:
- Redwood bench & perf: Ben Carter (ben_carter@redwood.com)
- Redwood SRE & infra: Monica Patel (monica_patel@redwood.com)
- Lumenitas POC lead: Alyssa Green (alyssa.green@lumenitas.ai)

Please confirm the dataset delivery date and whether you prefer Redwood to provision a temporary VPC endpoint or use hosted sandbox API keys (we recommend VPC for realistic network latency).

Monica

--
Monica Patel

Redwood Inference
From: Alyssa Green <alyssa.green@lumenitas.ai>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Devon Chu <devon.chu@lumenitas.ai>
Date: Fri, 23 Jan 2026 08:25:00 -0800
Subject: Re: POC acceptance checklist & Lumenitas sandbox next steps

Rafael / Ben / Monica — thanks for the clear plan. We reviewed internally and agree to the proposed acceptance slate with two small tweaks:
1) Quality pass threshold: raise to 91% (from 90%) for the seed set we share — our product team is conservative on regression risk.
2) Cost target: allow a 10% buffer on the initial projection to account for bursty indexing in week 1.

We will:
- Share the anonymized 24-hour traffic trace + tokenizer config by EOD Friday (1/23).
- Upload the seed test set to the Drive folder (link below).
- Confirm we prefer Redwood to provision a temporary VPC endpoint for the sandbox.

Confirming next meeting: technical kickoff Tuesday 1/27 at 10:00am PT (let us know if that needs to move).

Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1LumenitasPOC
HubSpot: HS-452199

Thanks — looking forward to the kickoff.

Alyssa Green

Lumenitas

source.subject: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management
source.body:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 9:08 AM PST
Subject: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Team — sharing a quick exec update on the serving-runtime tail latency workstream (KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction) since it’s now gating both SLO confidence and a couple of late-stage conversations.

Why this matters (business + technical):
- SLO / reliability: We’re seeing burst-driven p95/p99 spikes in sustained decode workloads with mixed sequence lengths, especially when request churn leaves the KV arena fragmented. In the worst case it creates opportunistic evictions at bad times and can tip us into OOM / request shedding.
- Cost: Today we’re compensating by leaving more headroom than we want (lower effective utilization). If we can keep KV memory contiguous/predictable under load, we should need less “safety padding” in dedicated pools and can be more aggressive on hosted concurrency without tail regressions.
- Customer pressure: This pattern shows up repeatedly in support and in enterprise eval questions (see SUP-28411, SUP-28433). PM has asked for a credible plan and crisp risk story.

What we’re doing:
- Allocator + fragmentation measurement: improve page/block layout + free-list management; add a fragmentation ratio metric that correlates with p99 spikes.
- Budgeted compaction: incremental compaction with an explicit time/bytes budget tied to scheduler boundaries (prefill/decode transitions) to avoid decode stalls.
- Tier-aware eviction: last-resort pressure relief that is safe + observable; interacts cleanly with pinned sessions.
- Guardrails: feature flag, kill-switch, backpressure integration, and rollout policy that auto-rolls back on tail regression.

Timeline (targeting runtime-1.22 train):
- Jan 20–31: discovery/measurement + final design/ADRs (in review now)
- Feb 3–21: implementation (allocator changes + scheduler hook + metrics + initial eviction policy)
- Feb 24–Mar 7: validation (benchmark lab + perf canary + stress/conformance)
- Mar 10+: staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → dedicated pools)

Risk management (what I want exec eyes on):
- Primary risk is introducing copy overhead / stalls during compaction. Mitigation is strict budgeting and scheduler-phase enforcement.
- Secondary risk is correctness edge cases (cancelled requests mid-relocation, pinned sessions). Mitigation is stress tests + conservative stopping conditions + kill-switch.
- We will hold rollout if: p99 up > agreed threshold, eviction rate spikes, or compaction time/bytes moved exceeds budget in steady state.

Asks:
1) Mateo/Sean: please confirm you’re aligned on “stop the line” thresholds + who has authority to flip the kill-switch during a canary.
2) Connor/Talia: I’d like this treated as a first-class item on the runtime-1.22 release readiness checklist.
3) Starting next week: weekly status note to this thread (wins, risks, current metrics, next gates). Keep it short.

Attachments:
- KV_Compaction_Review_Slides_v3.pdf
- Runtime_1.22_Rollout_Guardrails_Summary.docx

Ava Chen
VP, Product & Platform
Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Aligned.

A couple notes to help frame risk:
- We are not doing “stop-the-world” defragmentation. Compaction is incremental with a hard budget (bytes moved + wall time) and can no-op if we’re in a latency-sensitive phase.
- Eviction is a pressure valve, not the primary mechanism. We expect it to be near-zero in steady state; if eviction is non-trivial, that’s a signal we should treat as capacity/traffic policy rather than “normal.”
- Omar + Sofia are owning correctness + scheduler integration; Noah is doing architecture/correctness review on the invariants.

Re: timeline, I think Feb implementation window is realistic provided we keep scope to allocator + minimal copy/pack kernels.

Mateo Alvarez
VP Runtime
Redwood Inference


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

+1.

On the guardrails / authority question:
- During canary, oncall SRE has authority to flip the kill-switch if we hit rollback criteria (p99 sustained breach + compaction/eviction correlated) without waiting for runtime to be awake. Runtime gets paged/escalated in parallel.
- We’ll codify in the rollout policy: rollback on (a) p99 regression over baseline for N minutes, OR (b) compaction time budget violations + elevated tail, OR (c) eviction rate > threshold for N minutes.

Allison + Rafael are helping make sure the dashboards/alerts are in place before we expand beyond internal canary.

Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>

title: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 9:08 AM PST
Subject: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Team — sharing a quick exec update on the serving-runtime tail latency workstream (KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction) since it’s now gating both SLO confidence and a couple of late-stage conversations.

Why this matters (business + technical):
- SLO / reliability: We’re seeing burst-driven p95/p99 spikes in sustained decode workloads with mixed sequence lengths, especially when request churn leaves the KV arena fragmented. In the worst case it creates opportunistic evictions at bad times and can tip us into OOM / request shedding.
- Cost: Today we’re compensating by leaving more headroom than we want (lower effective utilization). If we can keep KV memory contiguous/predictable under load, we should need less “safety padding” in dedicated pools and can be more aggressive on hosted concurrency without tail regressions.
- Customer pressure: This pattern shows up repeatedly in support and in enterprise eval questions (see SUP-28411, SUP-28433). PM has asked for a credible plan and crisp risk story.

What we’re doing:
- Allocator + fragmentation measurement: improve page/block layout + free-list management; add a fragmentation ratio metric that correlates with p99 spikes.
- Budgeted compaction: incremental compaction with an explicit time/bytes budget tied to scheduler boundaries (prefill/decode transitions) to avoid decode stalls.
- Tier-aware eviction: last-resort pressure relief that is safe + observable; interacts cleanly with pinned sessions.
- Guardrails: feature flag, kill-switch, backpressure integration, and rollout policy that auto-rolls back on tail regression.

Timeline (targeting runtime-1.22 train):
- Jan 20–31: discovery/measurement + final design/ADRs (in review now)
- Feb 3–21: implementation (allocator changes + scheduler hook + metrics + initial eviction policy)
- Feb 24–Mar 7: validation (benchmark lab + perf canary + stress/conformance)
- Mar 10+: staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → dedicated pools)

Risk management (what I want exec eyes on):
- Primary risk is introducing copy overhead / stalls during compaction. Mitigation is strict budgeting and scheduler-phase enforcement.
- Secondary risk is correctness edge cases (cancelled requests mid-relocation, pinned sessions). Mitigation is stress tests + conservative stopping conditions + kill-switch.
- We will hold rollout if: p99 up > agreed threshold, eviction rate spikes, or compaction time/bytes moved exceeds budget in steady state.

Asks:
1) Mateo/Sean: please confirm you’re aligned on “stop the line” thresholds + who has authority to flip the kill-switch during a canary.
2) Connor/Talia: I’d like this treated as a first-class item on the runtime-1.22 release readiness checklist.
3) Starting next week: weekly status note to this thread (wins, risks, current metrics, next gates). Keep it short.

Attachments:
- KV_Compaction_Review_Slides_v3.pdf
- Runtime_1.22_Rollout_Guardrails_Summary.docx

Ava Chen
VP, Product & Platform
Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Aligned.

A couple notes to help frame risk:
- We are not doing “stop-the-world” defragmentation. Compaction is incremental with a hard budget (bytes moved + wall time) and can no-op if we’re in a latency-sensitive phase.
- Eviction is a pressure valve, not the primary mechanism. We expect it to be near-zero in steady state; if eviction is non-trivial, that’s a signal we should treat as capacity/traffic policy rather than “normal.”
- Omar + Sofia are owning correctness + scheduler integration; Noah is doing architecture/correctness review on the invariants.

Re: timeline, I think Feb implementation window is realistic provided we keep scope to allocator + minimal copy/pack kernels.

Mateo Alvarez
VP Runtime
Redwood Inference


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

+1.

On the guardrails / authority question:
- During canary, oncall SRE has authority to flip the kill-switch if we hit rollback criteria (p99 sustained breach + compaction/eviction correlated) without waiting for runtime to be awake. Runtime gets paged/escalated in parallel.
- We’ll codify in the rollout policy: rollback on (a) p99 regression over baseline for N minutes, OR (b) compaction time budget violations + elevated tail, OR (c) eviction rate > threshold for N minutes.

Allison + Rafael are helping make sure the dashboards/alerts are in place before we expand beyond internal canary.

Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 12:41 PM PST

source.subject: Capacity on-ramp: procurement playbook & vendor timelines (Acme AI)
messages:
From: Sofia Alvarez <sofia@acme.ai>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@acme.ai>
Date: Mon, 12 Jan 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: Initial ask: procurement timelines & OEM/cloud options for Dedicated pool

Priya / Karthik —

Thanks again for the demo last week. We’re moving fast on the RDW-4382 deal and procurement is asking for concrete timelines so we can align our internal release. High-level asks from Acme:

- Target on-ramp start: mid-April 2026 (preferred)
- Required steady state capacity: ~220 concurrent sessions equivalent (estimate attached)
- Preference: dedicated on-prem/GCP reserved mix — we need OEM quotes for hardware lead-time and a parallel cloud commitment timeline.

Could you share:
1) Typical OEM lead-times (order -> delivery) for an 8–16 node dedicated cluster with H100/A100 options.
2) Cloud reserved capacity lead times and whether committed capacity can be reserved within 2–4 weeks.
3) A short mutual action plan (MAP) template and any PO template we can use to speed procurement.

Attached is our internal sizing sheet (RFP_Attachment.pdf) that shows expected token rates and concurrency.

Appreciate a short playbook we can hand to procurement.

— Sofia
Sofia Alvarez
Head of Platform @ Acme AI
sofia@acme.ai

(Attachment: RFP_Attachment.pdf)

From: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Sofia Alvarez <sofia@acme.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 13 Jan 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial ask: procurement timelines & OEM/cloud options for Dedicated pool

Sofia — thanks for the clear asks and the RFP. We'll put together a short procurement playbook and MAP you can hand to your Sourcing team. Quick summary ahead of the full doc:

- Owners: Priya (vendor + procurement coordination), Karthik (sizing & runbook), Ben (commercial lead), Rafael (vendor ops / PO coordination).
- Rough timeline (targeted): Procurement kickoff this week -> OEM quote & terms by Jan 30 -> PO & payment terms agreed by Feb 14 -> hardware shipment starting Mar 15 -> in-house acceptance window Apr 12–20.
- Two-track approach: 1) OEM hardware (capex) lead with a staged delivery, 2) Cloud reserved burst capacity (short-term) to meet mid-April if any OEM delays.

Next steps from our side:
1) Karthik will send a sizing breakdown (node count / part numbers) + preferred vendor SKUs.
2) Rafael will collect OEM quotes and provide a sample PO template.
3) Ben will draft commercial terms for the reserved cloud fallback and early-bird discount notes.

I’ll attach a procurement checklist and the MAP draft in the follow-up.

Best,
Priya Natarajan
Head of Strategic Accounts, Redwood Inference
(Attachment: Procurement_Checklist.docx)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Sofia Alvarez <sofia@acme.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Jan 2026 14:21:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial ask: procurement timelines & OEM/cloud options for Dedicated pool

Sofia / Priya / Ben —

Sizing & vendor-specific notes (summary):

- Target workload: generative chat + embeddings. Baseline LLMs: 70–120 token responses, bursty peaks.
- Recommended hardware profiles (tradeoffs):
  * Option A (throughput-oriented): 8 x NVIDIA H100 80GB per pool, dual-socket host, 1 x 200GbE fabric; expected per-pool steady throughput ~1.2k tokens/s at typical batching.
  * Option B (cost-capacity mix): 12 x A100 80GB (if A100 inventory is available), slightly lower perf per card but cheaper lead-time in some OEM channels.

- Node counts for your stated concurrency (~220 sessions): we recommend starting with 2 pools:
  * Pool-1 (latency-sensitive): 4 H100 nodes (can serve SLO-critical endpoints)
  * Pool-2 (throughput/cheap): 8 A100 nodes for batch workloads and embeddings.

- Lead-time expectations (empirical):
  * OEM direct (Dell / Supermicro / Lambda): 8–14 weeks after PO (variant by SKU and customs), typical lead time for H100 systems = 10–12 weeks.
  * Channel/reseller backlog can add 2–4 weeks.
  * Cloud reserved/private tenancy (GCP/ AWS Dedicated Host commitments): 2–6 weeks to provision committed capacity + networking setup; shorter (2–3 weeks) if we pre-negotiate commitments and provide templates.

- Contingency: For mid-April on-ramp, plan for a 50% cloud-backed reserved pool for the first 6 weeks. We can autoscale to OEM hardware as it arrives.

Attached: a quick SLA/capacity workbook with sample node mappings and cost tradeoffs.

Karthik
Staff Engineer, Inference Platform
(Attachment: CloudCapacity_SLA_v3.xlsx)

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Sofia Alvarez <sofia@acme.ai>
Date: Fri, 16 Jan 2026 11:46:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial ask: procurement timelines & OEM/cloud options for Dedicated pool

All — adding vendor ops details and a draft quote bundle. Key operational points: 

- OEM quotes: I engaged Dell and a regional Supermicro partner. Dell has an 8–10 week ETA for full H100 racks but has a 4–6 week window for staged shipments if we accept partial configurations. Supermicro quoted 10–14 weeks with customs buffer. I’ve attached the consolidated quote file (OEM_Quotes_Q1_2026.pdf).

- PO templates & payment terms: Our standard PO template (attached) has Net 30/Net 45 options; OEMs often require 30% deposit on order for custom SKUs. For Acme, we can propose a 20% deposit tied to an acceptance schedule to speed delivery.

- Cloud handoff: GCP account team indicated they can provision committed nodes in ~2 weeks if we sign a short-term commitment and provide SOW + IP/Network contacts. We should prepare a one-page SOW and a DB redaction affidavit for compliance.

- Risks & mitigations:
  1) SKU shortages — mitigation: accept mixed-node staging (A100 fallback) + temporary cloud burst.
  2) Customs delays — mitigation: prioritize domestic channel partners where possible.

I’ll schedule a vendor sync next Tue (Jan 20) at 10:00 PT to align Dell / Supermicro reps and GCP. Please review the quotes and flag any commercial constraints.

Rafael Mendes
Vendor Ops — Redwood Inference
(Attachment: OEM_Quotes_Q1_2026.pdf)

From: Sofia Alvarez <sofia@acme.ai>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@acme.ai>
Date: Tue, 20 Jan 2026 16:27:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial ask: procurement timelines & OEM/cloud options for Dedicated pool

Priya / Rafael / team —

This is very helpful — thanks. A few notes from Acme Procurement after an internal review: 

- Acme prefers the staged-shipment proposal (partial A100/H100 mix) to mitigate single-SKU risk.
- Procurement asked for a formal MAP and a signed PO template they can use for internal approvals. Can you share the DocuSign-ready MAP and the PO with the 20% deposit proposal?
- We’ll join the vendor sync on Jan 20 at 10:00 PT — Jordan will be the procurement lead on our side.

If we can get the DocuSign link and MAP today, we can route approvals this week and hit the Feb 14 PO target. Quoting the earlier message: "Short-term cloud reserved pool for mid-April" is acceptable as a fallback.

Thanks — looking forward to the vendor sync.

— Sofia
Sofia Alvarez
Head of Platform, Acme AI
(Quoted: "On Fri, 16 Jan 2026, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com> wrote:")

  * Option A (throughput-oriented): 8 x NVIDIA H100 80GB per pool, dual-socket host, 1 x 200GbE fabric; expected per-pool steady throughput ~1.2k tokens/s at typical batching.
  * Option B (cost-capacity mix): 12 x A100 80GB (if A100 inventory is available), slightly lower perf per card but cheaper lead-time in some OEM channels.

- Node counts for your stated concurrency (~220 sessions): we recommend starting with 2 pools:
  * Pool-1 (latency-sensitive): 4 H100 nodes (can serve SLO-critical endpoints)
  * Pool-2 (throughput/cheap): 8 A100 nodes for batch workloads and embeddings.

- Lead-time expectations (empirical):
  * OEM direct (Dell / Supermicro / Lambda): 8–14 weeks after PO (variant by SKU and customs), typical lead time for H100 systems = 10–12 weeks.
  * Channel/reseller backlog can add 2–4 weeks.
  * Cloud reserved/private tenancy (GCP/ AWS Dedicated Host commitments): 2–6 weeks to provision committed capacity + networking setup; shorter (2–3 weeks) if we pre-negotiate commitments and provide templates.

- Contingency: For mid-April on-ramp, plan for a 50% cloud-backed reserved pool for the first 6 weeks. We can autoscale to OEM hardware as it arrives.

Attached: a quick SLA/capacity workbook with sample node mappings and cost tradeoffs.

Karthik
Staff Engineer, Inference Platform

source.subject: Proposed DPA: Incident notification timelines and subprocessor cooperation
messages:
From: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Date: 2026-01-18T09:12:00-05:00
Subject: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Rafael / team,

Thanks for sending the initial DPA draft. Before we move to signatures our legal and security teams want to clarify the incident notification language. Two items of particular concern: the notification timeline and what Redwood will commit to around "cooperation + evidence" (forensics, logs, impacted datasets).

High level asks from BluePeak:
- Initial notification for a confirmed breach within 24 hours of confirmation.
- A more detailed incident report (impact, root cause, remediation plan) within 5 business days.
- Subprocessor flow-down: Redwood must require subprocessors to notify BluePeak directly or provide proof of notification within the same timeframe.
- For cross-border data, we need confirmation that any transfers will be covered by SCCs or equivalent.

Attached is a short questionnaire our security team uses (BP_security_qs.pdf). Can you confirm if Redwood can meet the 24/5 schedule, or if you have alternative wording we should consider? We can also loop in our technical contact for a short call if helpful.

Thanks,
Anna
Anna Kim
Head of Security, BluePeak Analytics
anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com
+1 415 555 0104

Attachment: BP_security_qs.pdf (PDF, 72KB)

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Date: 2026-01-18T11:03:00-05:00
Subject: Re: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Anna — thanks for the clear list. We're aligned on the importance of fast notification and transparency. A few clarifying points from our side:

1) "Initial notification" definitions vary across vendors. We typically commit to initial notification within 48 hours of confirming an incident that affects customer data, with an interim acknowledgement sooner if confirmation is pending.
2) A detailed root-cause and remediation report within 5 business days is something we can do in many cases; for complex forensic analysis we may need a follow-up window (we usually aim for 10 business days for a full forensic packet).
3) Subprocessors: Redwood requires all subprocessors to flow down notification obligations. We prefer to provide proof of flow-down and any subprocessor notifications (redacted as necessary) rather than creating a contractual right for every customer to claim direct notice from subprocessors.
4) Cross-border transfers: Redwood uses SCCs where required and can provide references to our DPA exhibit.

I've attached a redline with our proposed incident-notice language and a couple of options (48/72 vs. 24/5). Please review and tell us which direction is needed to proceed with signatures.

Proposed next step: short sync between our compliance lead (Neha) and your counsel/infosec to agree language. Are you available tomorrow afternoon?

Best,
Rafael Mendes
Head of Strategic Accounts (Americas)
Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com | +1 650 555 0199

Attachment: DPA_Redline_Redwood_v1.docx (Word, redline)

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>, Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-01-19T09:20:00-05:00
Subject: RE: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Hi Anna / Laura — looping in a bit of suggested clause text to narrow the gap. Below are two options and a short explanation. Happy to review live.

Option A (balanced, preferred by Redwood):
"Incident Notification. Upon discovery or receipt of credible evidence of an unauthorized access to Customer Data, Provider shall: (a) provide Customer with an initial notification describing the nature of the incident, the categories of data reasonably believed to be affected, and any immediate mitigation steps taken, within forty-eight (48) hours of Provider's confirmation of the incident; (b) provide a substantive incident report within five (5) business days of Provider's confirmation, and a final forensic report within ten (10) business days if warranted; and (c) cooperate with Customer and provide reasonable evidence (e.g., logs, timelines, remediation steps) consistent with applicable law and confidentiality obligations."

Option B (customer-favored):
"Incident Notification. For confirmed incidents impacting Customer Data, Provider shall notify Customer without undue delay and in any event within twenty-four (24) hours of confirmation. Provider shall deliver a comprehensive incident report within five (5) business days and cooperate in all reasonable regulatory inquiries."

Subprocessor flow-down: Redwood will require subprocessors to accept materially equivalent obligations and will provide confirmation of such flow-down within five (5) business days of a Customer request; where a subprocessor notifies Provider directly, Provider will forward such notice to Customer within the same timeframe as Provider's own notice.

Evidence and cooperation: Provider will provide reasonable access to forensic summaries, event timelines, and root-cause analysis, subject to redaction obligations and third-party privacy/confidentiality restrictions. Provider will also provide a named technical contact and an escalation path (including a PagerDuty/phone number) for high-severity incidents.

We put the 48-hour initial window in Option A because very early notifications sometimes contain unvetted/incorrect conclusions that can create downstream regulatory noise. Option A gives both speed and a short period for confirmation. If BluePeak requires 24 hours for confirmed incidents, we can accept Option B but will need to carve out an exception for incidents that require additional investigation to confirm scope.

Ping me with availability for a 30-minute call tomorrow (Neha) to finalize language.

Regards,
Neha Kapoor
Head of Compliance, Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com

From: Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>
Date: 2026-01-19T15:47:00-05:00
Subject: Re: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Thanks Neha — helpful framing. A few counterpoints from BluePeak's legal position:

- We are comfortable distinguishing between "confirmed" and "suspected" incidents. For confirmed incidents we will insist on a 24-hour notification (our regulators expect prompt disclosure once data breach is confirmed). For suspected incidents, 72 hours to provide an initial update is acceptable.
- For subprocessors, BluePeak's preference is that Redwood: (i) include a contractual obligation that requires subprocessors to notify Provider within 24 hours of confirmation; and (ii) provide direct evidence of notification (a copy of the subprocessor notice or confirmation) to BluePeak upon Customer request within five (5) business days.
- Regarding evidence: we need at minimum redacted logs showing access patterns, impacted object identifiers, and confirmed user accounts affected. Where logs are subject to third-party restrictions, Redwood should describe the limitation and provide alternative evidence.
- On cross-border transfers: BluePeak needs a statement that transfers will be covered by SCCs (or equivalent) and that Redwood will promptly provide copies of executed SCCs or other transfer mechanisms upon request.

Attached is a redline of your Option A with our revisions (DPA_redline_bp_v2.docx). If Redwood can accept: 24h for confirmed, 72h for suspected, subprocessors notify Provider within 24h and Provider to forward within 5 business days, we can proceed to final sign-off after a short exec confirmation.

-LJ

Laura Jensen
Counsel, BluePeak Analytics
laura.jensen@blegal.com

Attachment: DPA_redline_bp_v2.docx (Word, redline)

On Mon, Jan 19, 2026 at 09:20 Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Hi Anna / Laura — looping in a bit of suggested clause text to narrow the gap...

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>, Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-01-20T14:30:00-05:00
Subject: Re: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Team — thanks for the constructive edits. Proposed mutual-action summary below so we have a clear path to finalize:

1) Initial notification: 24 hours for confirmed incidents; 72 hours for suspected incidents (agreed).
2) Subprocessors: Redwood will require subprocessors to notify Redwood within 24 hours of confirmation and will forward any subprocessor notices and/or confirmation of flow-down to BluePeak within five (5) business days. Redwood will also provide written proof of flow-down upon request.
3) Reports & evidence: Redwood will provide a substantive incident report within five (5) business days and follow-up forensic details within ten (10) business days when required. Provided evidence will include redacted logs, impacted object identifiers, and affected user account information to the extent not prohibited by third-party obligations.
4) Transfers & SCCs: Redwood confirms SCCs or equivalent transfer mechanisms will be used for cross-border processing and executed copies will be provided upon Customer request.

Next step: Rafael to book a 30-minute call between Neha, Laura, Anna, and a technical lead (we'll include an on-call contact) to sign off on the final redline and get this into DocuSign. Proposed times: Wed 2026-01-21 10:00-10:30 PT or 14:00-14:30 PT. Please confirm.

If you prefer, we can also accept the attached BluePeak redline with the agreed edits and we will prepare the final DPA for signatures.

Appreciate the collaboration — happy to lock this down quickly.

Best,
Rafael Mendes
Head of Strategic Accounts (Americas)
Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com

Attached is a redline of your Option A with our revisions (DPA_redline_bp_v2.docx). If Redwood can accept: 24h for confirmed, 72h for suspected, subprocessors notify Provider within 24h and Provider to forward within 5 business days, we can proceed to final sign-off after a short exec confirmation.

-LJ

Laura Jensen
Counsel, BluePeak Analytics
laura.jensen@blegal.com

Attachment: DPA_redline_bp_v2.docx (Word, redline)

On Mon, Jan 19, 2026 at 09:20 Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Hi Anna / Laura — looping in a bit of suggested clause text to narrow the gap...

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>, Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-01-20T14:30:00-05:00
Subject: Re: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Team — thanks for the constructive edits. Proposed mutual-action summary below so we have a clear path to finalize:

1) Initial notification: 24 hours for confirmed incidents; 72 hours for suspected incidents (agreed).
2) Subprocessors: Redwood will require subprocessors to notify Redwood within 24 hours of confirmation and will forward any subprocessor notices and/or confirmation of flow-down to BluePeak within five (5) business days. Redwood will also provide written proof of flow-down upon request.
3) Reports & evidence: Redwood will provide a substantive incident report within five (5) business days and follow-up forensic details within ten (10) business days when required. Provided evidence will include redacted logs, impacted object identifiers, and affected user account information to the extent not prohibited by third-party obligations.
4) Transfers & SCCs: Redwood confirms SCCs or equivalent transfer mechanisms will be used for cross-border processing and executed copies will be provided upon Customer request.

Next step: Rafael to book a 30-minute call between Neha, Laura, Anna, and a technical lead (we'll include an on-call contact) to sign off on the final redline and get this into DocuSign. Proposed times: Wed 2026-01-21 10:00-10:30 PT or 14:00-14:30 PT. Please confirm.

If you prefer, we can also accept the attached BluePeak redline with the agreed edits and we will prepare the final DPA for signatures.

Appreciate the collaboration — happy to lock this down quickly.

Best,
Rafael Mendes
Head of Strategic Accounts (Americas)
Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com
text:
From: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Date: 2026-01-18T09:12:00-05:00
Subject: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Rafael / team,

Thanks for sending the initial DPA draft. Before we move to signatures our legal and security teams want to clarify the incident notification language. Two items of particular concern: the notification timeline and what Redwood will commit to around "cooperation + evidence" (forensics, logs, impacted datasets).

High level asks from BluePeak:
- Initial notification for a confirmed breach within 24 hours of confirmation.
- A more detailed incident report (impact, root cause, remediation plan) within 5 business days.
- Subprocessor flow-down: Redwood must require subprocessors to notify BluePeak directly or provide proof of notification within the same timeframe.
- For cross-border data, we need confirmation that any transfers will be covered by SCCs or equivalent.

Attached is a short questionnaire our security team uses (BP_security_qs.pdf). Can you confirm if Redwood can meet the 24/5 schedule, or if you have alternative wording we should consider? We can also loop in our technical contact for a short call if helpful.

Thanks,
Anna
Anna Kim
Head of Security, BluePeak Analytics
anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com
+1 415 555 0104

Attachment: BP_security_qs.pdf (PDF, 72KB)

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Date: 2026-01-18T11:03:00-05:00
Subject: Re: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Anna — thanks for the clear list. We're aligned on the importance of fast notification and transparency. A few clarifying points from our side:

1) "Initial notification" definitions vary across vendors. We typically commit to initial notification within 48 hours of confirming an incident that affects customer data, with an interim acknowledgement sooner if confirmation is pending.
2) A detailed root-cause and remediation report within 5 business days is something we can do in many cases; for complex forensic analysis we may need a follow-up window (we usually aim for 10 business days for a full forensic packet).
3) Subprocessors: Redwood requires all subprocessors to flow down notification obligations. We prefer to provide proof of flow-down and any subprocessor notifications (redacted as necessary) rather than creating a contractual right for every customer to claim direct notice from subprocessors.
4) Cross-border transfers: Redwood uses SCCs where required and can provide references to our DPA exhibit.

I've attached a redline with our proposed incident-notice language and a couple of options (48/72 vs. 24/5). Please review and tell us which direction is needed to proceed with signatures.

Proposed next step: short sync between our compliance lead (Neha) and your counsel/infosec to agree language. Are you available tomorrow afternoon?

Best,
Rafael Mendes
Head of Strategic Accounts (Americas)
Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com | +1 650 555 0199

Attachment: DPA_Redline_Redwood_v1.docx (Word, redline)

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>, Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-01-19T09:20:00-05:00
Subject: RE: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Hi Anna / Laura — looping in a bit of suggested clause text to narrow the gap. Below are two options and a short explanation. Happy to review live.

Option A (balanced, preferred by Redwood):
"Incident Notification. Upon discovery or receipt of credible evidence of an unauthorized access to Customer Data, Provider shall: (a) provide Customer with an initial notification describing the nature of the incident, the categories of data reasonably believed to be affected, and any immediate mitigation steps taken, within forty-eight (48) hours of Provider's confirmation of the incident; (b) provide a substantive incident report within five (5) business days of Provider's confirmation, and a final forensic report within ten (10) business days if warranted; and (c) cooperate with Customer and provide reasonable evidence (e.g., logs, timelines, remediation steps) consistent with applicable law and confidentiality obligations."

Option B (customer-favored):
"Incident Notification. For confirmed incidents impacting Customer Data, Provider shall notify Customer without undue delay and in any event within twenty-four (24) hours of confirmation. Provider shall deliver a comprehensive incident report within five (5) business days and cooperate in all reasonable regulatory inquiries."

Subprocessor flow-down: Redwood will require subprocessors to accept materially equivalent obligations and will provide confirmation of such flow-down within five (5) business days of a Customer request; where a subprocessor notifies Provider directly, Provider will forward such notice to Customer within the same timeframe as Provider's own notice.

Evidence and cooperation: Provider will provide reasonable access to forensic summaries, event timelines, and root-cause analysis, subject to redaction obligations and third-party privacy/confidentiality restrictions. Provider will also provide a named technical contact and an escalation path (including a PagerDuty/phone number) for high-severity incidents.

We put the 48-hour initial window in Option A because very early notifications sometimes contain unvetted/incorrect conclusions that can create downstream regulatory noise. Option A gives both speed and a short period for confirmation. If BluePeak requires 24 hours for confirmed incidents, we can accept Option B but will need to carve out an exception for incidents that require additional investigation to confirm scope.

Ping me with availability for a 30-minute call tomorrow (Neha) to finalize language.

Regards,
Neha Kapoor
Head of Compliance, Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com



From: Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@blegal.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Anna Kim <anna.kim@bluepeak-analytics.com>
Date: 2026-01-19T15:47:00-05:00
Subject: Re: DPA: incident notification expectations and evidence requests

Thanks Neha — helpful framing. A few counterpoints from BluePeak's legal position:

- We are comfortable distinguishing between "confirmed" and "suspected" incidents. For confirmed incidents we will insist on a 24-hour notification (our regulators expect prompt disclosure once data breach is confirmed). For suspected incidents, 72 hours to provide an initial update is acceptable.
- For subprocessors, BluePeak's preference is that Redwood: (i) include a contractual obligation that requires subprocessors to notify Provider within 24 hours of confirmation; and (ii) provide direct evidence of notification (a copy of the subprocessor notice or confirmation) to BluePeak upon Customer request within five (5) business days.
- Regarding evidence: we need at minimum redacted logs showing access patterns, impacted object identifiers, and confirmed user accounts affected. Where logs are subject to third-party restrictions, Redwood should describe the limitation and provide alternative evidence.
- On cross-border transfers: BluePeak needs a statement that transfers will be covered by SCCs (or equivalent) and that Redwood will promptly provide copies of executed SCCs or other transfer mechanisms upon request.

Attached is a redline of your Option A with our revisions (DPA_redline_bp_v2.docx). If Redwood can accept: 24h for confirmed, 72h for suspected, subprocessors notify Provider within 24h and Provider to forward within 5 business days, we can proceed to final sign-off after a short exec confirmation.

-LJ

Laura Jensen
Counsel, BluePeak Analytics
laura.jensen@blegal.com

Attachment: DPA_redline_bp_v2.docx (Word, redline)

source.subject: Request: Pilot onboarding without PO for HelixOps subcontractor
messages:
From: Samira Khan <samira.khan@helixops.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Pilot subvendor onboarding — can we proceed without PO?

Hi Connor,

Quick ask — we're running a small integration pilot with a HelixOps subcontractor (Lumen Labs) who'll be doing two weeks of instrumentation work for the integration. The engagement will be split across two invoices (~$1,800 each). Lumen is set up as a subcontractor under our agreement and cannot wait for a formal PO (their procurement cycle is much slower).

Is Redwood able to accept invoice-only onboarding for this pilot (no PO) so we can start next week? We can provide the W-9, insurance certificate, statement of work, and a signed subcontract. If needed, we can restrict it to a single invoice cap.

Appreciate a quick note on the path forward — timeline is tight.

Thanks,
Samira Khan
Vendor Ops, HelixOps
Samira.khan@helixops.com
+1 (415) 555-0123
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira.khan@helixops.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Pilot subvendor onboarding — can we proceed without PO?

Hi Samira — thanks for the heads up and timeline.

Short version: our standard is PO-first for vendor services, but Procurement maintains a narrow invoice-only path for small, time-sensitive pilots if we can get the right onboarding evidence and an approval from procurement + finance.

I've attached the vendor intake form you mentioned (please fill sections A–D). To move fast we'll need: 1) W-9, 2) certificate of insurance, 3) signed SOW, and 4) a primary POC for invoicing. Marissa (procurement) is cc'd to confirm any caps or additional evidence.

Proposed conditional path: one-time invoice-only onboarding capped at $4,999 for this pilot, with Lumen added to a temporary allowlist and invoices routed to Rafael in Finance for net-30 processing. If scope grows beyond the cap we will require a PO.

If that approach works, please return the completed intake form + docs and I'll flag it for procurement approval.

—Connor O'Brien
Senior Sales Manager | Redwood Inference
connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Samira Khan <samira.khan@helixops.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 16 Jan 2026 14:22:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Pilot subvendor onboarding — can we proceed without PO?

Thanks for the context. Procurement can support a narrow exception for pilot/subcontractor situations but we need a completed exception packet before we approve. Connor — please ask HelixOps to return the vendor intake form (attached) along with the items below: 

- Completed Vendor Intake Form (sections A–D)
- W-9 (or equivalent tax form)
- COI (certificate of insurance) naming Redwood Inference as additional insured if applicable
- Signed SOW or subcontract referencing the pilot scope and total not-to-exceed amount
- Primary invoicing email and remit address

Our approval criteria for invoice-only pilots: single invoice cap <= $4,999; exception valid for 30 days; Procurement will add the vendor to a temporary allowlist and mark the vendor record with “pilot-invoice-only”. Finance must provide GL/charge code and confirm net terms.

If HelixOps/Lumen agrees to the cap and provides paperwork by EOD Monday we'll route this for a fast-track sign-off. I'll attach the formal procurement exception form once Connor confirms.

Best,
Marissa Cole
Procurement Manager | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com
From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, 18 Jan 2026 08:47:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Pilot subvendor onboarding — can we proceed without PO?

On Fri, Jan 16, 2026 at 2:22 PM Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Our approval criteria for invoice-only pilots: single invoice cap <= $4,999; exception valid for 30 days; Procurement will add the vendor to a temporary allowlist and mark the vendor record with “pilot-invoice-only”. Finance must provide GL/charge code and confirm net terms.

I approve the one-time invoice-only exception for this pilot (cap $4,999). Conditions:
- All required docs must be on file before work begins (W-9, COI, signed SOW).
- Invoices must reference the HLX-2026-PILOT-47 deal id and be routed to finance@redwoodinference.com with cc to Connor.
- If total spend looks like it will exceed the cap, pause work and open a standard PO request.

Connor: please confirm HelixOps will return the intake form and docs and share expected invoice dates. Marissa/Rafael — please set up the temporary allowlist entry and GL code UAT-OPS-PILOT if that works for Finance.

Thanks —Laura
Laura Bennett
VP, Partnerships | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Jan 2026 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Pilot subvendor onboarding — can we proceed without PO?

Confirmed — finance has added Lumen Labs (subvendor for HelixOps) to a temporary vendor allowlist and set GL code UAT-OPS-PILOT. Payments will accept the two invoices under the one-time cap and will process net-30 once the intake packet is uploaded to VendorOnboard (see attached onboarding record).

Samira/HelixOps: please send final invoices to AP (payments@redwoodinference.com) and cc Connor. We'll release payment after standard validation. If you need a purchase order for future work, please note the cap will require a PO request.

Rafael Mendes
Director, Finance Operations | Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com

I approve the one-time invoice-only exception for this pilot (cap $4,999). Conditions:
- All required docs must be on file before work begins (W-9, COI, signed SOW).
- Invoices must reference the HLX-2026-PILOT-47 deal id and be routed to finance@redwoodinference.com with cc to Connor.
- If total spend looks like it will exceed the cap, pause work and open a standard PO request.

Connor: please confirm HelixOps will return the intake form and docs and share expected invoice dates. Marissa/Rafael — please set up the temporary allowlist entry and GL code UAT-OPS-PILOT if that works for Finance.

Thanks —Laura

laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com

Subject: SSO integration blocker — request for short extension & onboarding credits

Hi Olga,

Quick note from the CloudRentals infra team — we hit an unexpected blocker integrating our SSO provider with Redwood’s private deployment. Our engineering lead needs another sprint to complete the OIDC mapping and test logins. Because of the delay we won’t be able to complete the trial POC by the original expiry (Jan 21).

Can you help with: 1) a short trial extension (2–3 weeks) and 2) a small onboarding credit pool we can use during validation? The extra credits will let our QA run a full pass once SSO is in place without incurring unexpected costs.

We’re committed to finishing the technical work quickly — estimated by the end of next week — but need runway. Happy to jump on a 30-min call with your CS if that helps.

Hi Eva — thanks for the heads up and for outlining the timeline.

We can absolutely help. Proposed approach:
- Extend the trial by 21 days from the current expiry (new expiry: Feb 11).
- Provide a temporary onboarding credit pool of $4,000 to cover validation runs.
- Condition: we’d like to schedule a joint QA / onboarding sync next week so Monica can walk your team through the KV cache and rate-limit settings that commonly affect SSO-bound POCs.
