source.subject: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)
source.raw:
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Owen Phillips <owen.phillips@redwoodinference.com>, Wei Chen <wei.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

Sean/Rafa — sharing a quick Support summary on the status page granularity feedback we’ve been getting + confirming the escalation playbook and macros are ready to roll out once SRE signs off on the flow.

Top recurring customer complaints (last ~60 days; concentrated in enterprise accounts):

1) “Status page says API is OK, but embeddings are failing”
- Pattern: route/capability-specific failures (embeddings / reranking / streaming) not represented when the top-level “Hosted API” looks green.
- Representative ticket: SUP-28411 (missing embeddings component)

2) “Streaming disconnects aren’t reflected”
- Pattern: elevated disconnects/timeouts for streaming that don’t trip the same thresholds as request errors; customers experience it as a partial outage.
- Representative ticket: SUP-28549

3) Disputed status accuracy during partial/region-specific events
- Pattern: customer impact limited to us-east (or subset of capacity) but public status was either too coarse or declared too late.
- Representative ticket: SUP-28436

4) Time-to-first-update too slow / inconsistent
- Even when engineering is moving quickly, customers perceive silence in the first ~20–40 minutes.
- Ask: “Can you at least acknowledge investigation?” (even without RCA)

5) Inconsistent post-incident summaries
- Ask: publishable summary within 24–72 hours with impact window, what changed, and prevention, without over-sharing.
- Representative ticket: SUP-28502

What Support needs to be successful (what we’re implementing on our side):

A) A clear escalation path when a customer disputes status accuracy
- Who do we page / what evidence do we include (dashboards, timestamps, affected routes)?
- When do we tell the customer “we’ll confirm and update status if warranted” vs “this is customer-specific”?

B) Macros that match the new incident comms templates
- We’re standardizing language so Support doesn’t invent phrasing during an incident.
- We will link to the public incident + post-incident summary when available.

We drafted the Support playbook and macros to align to the new comms phases (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved). Proposed rollout:
- Playbook: Confluence “Status page customer escalation playbook” (internal)
- Macros: update in Support tooling the same day the comms templates go live
- Training: 20 min enablement in #support + office hours with SRE

Open items for SRE confirmation:
1) Confirm who is the designated owner when Support escalates a “status accuracy dispute” during an active incident (IC vs Comms Lead vs SRE on-call).
2) Confirm the “don’t say” list (Dedicated/Private, customer-specific capacity) so Support stays inside the rails.
3) Confirm target response time for Support escalations during Sev0/Sev1 (even if the answer is “acknowledged, investigating impact scope”).

If you’re OK with it, we’ll proceed with rollout this week tied to PM-611/PM-612.

Thanks,
Dev Patel
Director, Support Operations
Redwood Inference


---

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Owen Phillips <owen.phillips@redwoodinference.com>, Wei Chen <wei.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 10:01 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

This matches what we’ve seen on the SRE side.

Answers to your open items (draft; Sean can confirm final):

1) Owner for “status accuracy dispute” during an active incident:
- Primary: Comms Lead (if assigned)
- Backup: Incident Commander
- If neither responds within 10 minutes: SRE on-call via paging policy “SRE-INC-COMMS”

2) Don’t say list (customer-specific capacity / Dedicated/Private):
- Agree. We should keep Support language to “we’re investigating whether there is a broader service impact” and avoid confirming anything that implies a single customer’s isolation is compromised.
- For Dedicated/Private incidents, Support can say: “We’re investigating. Some issues may be isolated to specific deployments; we’ll share a public update if there is a multi-tenant or broadly scoped impact.”

3) Target response time:
- For Sev0/Sev1: acknowledge within 15 minutes (internally to Support), even if status page update is still being validated.

We can also provide a short checklist of what evidence to include:
- region, capability (streaming/embeddings/reranking/chat), request IDs if available, error class, timestamps, and which dashboards you checked.

Rafa


---

From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Owen Phillips <owen.phillips@redwoodinference.com>, Wei Chen <wei.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 10:26 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

+1. From CS perspective, the biggest friction is when we can’t point customers to something canonical during the first hour.

Two small asks for the macros/playbook:
- Include a line for CS to set expectation on next update time (“we’ll share another update within X minutes”) without promising a fix.
- Include a sentence about workarounds/fallback behavior *only if* IC/Comms Lead confirms it’s safe (we’ve accidentally implied “failover” where none existed).

Rachel and I can help pilot this in our enterprise book once the macros are live.

Jasmine


---

From: Wei Chen <wei.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Owen Phillips <owen.phillips@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 11:07 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

I reviewed the proposed macro language in your draft (thank you for keeping it tight).

Tone tweaks I’d recommend (to align with the incident templates):
- Use “We are investigating reports of…” vs “We are experiencing…” in the first message if scope isn’t confirmed.
- Avoid “SLA” unless Legal has explicitly approved for that account.
- Replace “outage” with “service disruption” unless it’s clearly total unavailability.

If you share the final macro blocks, I can do a quick pass to ensure we’re consistent with the public template phrasing.

Wei


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Owen Phillips <owen.phillips@redwoodinference.com>, Wei Chen <wei.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 12:14 PM PT
Subject: Re: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

Approved to proceed.

Decisions for the playbook/macros (effective immediately for the rollout):

1) Escalation owner
- Comms Lead is the primary owner for “status accuracy dispute” escalations during an active incident.
- IC is responsible for ensuring Comms Lead is assigned; if no Comms Lead is assigned yet, IC owns the escalation until assignment.

2) Response time commitment (internal)
- Sev0/Sev1: Support escalations acknowledged by Comms Lead or IC within 15 minutes.
- Sev2+: acknowledged within 30 minutes during business hours; best-effort otherwise.

3) Public vs customer-specific
- If impact is plausibly broad (multi-tenant, region, capability), Comms Lead should assess and update public component(s) once validated.
- If impact is customer-specific, Support message should explicitly say “we’re investigating your reports” and route customer through Support for updates (do not imply broad incident).

4) Content constraints
- Do not reference customer-specific capacity, dedicated hostnames, or deployment topology.
- Avoid promising fix timelines.

Dev — please roll the macros and playbook this week and schedule the 20-minute training. Rafa can join for Q&A.

Sean


---

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Owen Phillips <owen.phillips@redwoodinference.com>, Wei Chen <wei.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Nov 19, 2025 at 10:03 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

Thanks all — we’ll proceed.

Next steps (Support):
- Publish playbook page today (with the owner/response-time decisions Sean listed).
- Update macros in tooling by EOD Thu (aligned to the incident template phases).
- Training: Fri 11:00–11:20 PT (invite coming). We’ll include examples from SUP-28411 / SUP-28549 so agents can map symptoms to “capability + region” and escalate cleanly.

Wei — I’ll send you the final macro blocks this afternoon for tone pass.

Dev
Director, Support Operations
Redwood Inference

title: Status page granularity: top customer complaints + Support escalation playbook/macros (ready for rollout)

source.subject: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)
source.body:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 9:08 AM
Subject: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Team —

As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we want Console to link customers to (a) active incidents and (b) post-incident summaries once published.

What I’m trying to lock down today:
1) What we can show/link in Console for Hosted API incidents (public status page) vs Dedicated vs Private.
2) Any constraints for incident visibility (don’t leak customer-specific capacity issues, don’t imply SLA breach, etc.).
3) A minimum v1 scope that we can ship with the component taxonomy rollout.

Proposed UX (v1):
- Add a “Status” entry in the Console nav.
- If there is an active incident affecting the customer’s selected project/region, show a banner on relevant pages + a “View incident details” link.
- “Status” page lists:
  - Current component health (from public status where applicable)
  - Recent incidents (last 30 days) with links to the incident page
  - Post-incident summaries (same list; shown when available)

Open question (big one): Dedicated/Private.
- Hosted: straightforward (public status page + public summaries).
- Dedicated: we need guidance on whether we (a) link to public incident objects, (b) show something tenant-specific in Console, or (c) keep Dedicated as a generic “Control Plane / Provisioning” component only.
- Private: my assumption is no public status page. Do we show anything in Console beyond a generic “contact support” if we know the Private deployment is impacted?

Can folks reply with constraints + recommendation by EOD? I want to feed a crisp decision into DES-244/PM-612 and keep Nora unblocked on implementation.

Marissa
Product, Console & Rollouts


---

From: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 9:37 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

+1 on Hosted being easy.

Dedicated/Private constraints (current policy direction from PM-612 discussions):
- Dedicated incidents are not always “public,” because the blast radius can be one customer or a small set of customers. We should not post customer-specific Dedicated capacity constraints on the public status page.
- Private: same, but stricter. Many Private deployments are in customer-controlled environments; posting statuses publicly creates confusion and can disclose that a particular customer is running us.

Recommendation (v1):
- Hosted: link directly to status.redwoodinference.com incident + post-incident summary.
- Dedicated:
  - Public status page only for Dedicated *control plane* (provisioning, console auth to dedicated mgmt, etc.) when it is truly shared.
  - Customer-specific Dedicated data plane issues should show in Console only for the impacted org (banner + short text), and should not link to a public incident.
- Private:
  - No public incidents.
  - Console can show a generic banner only when we are confident it’s a Redwood-managed control plane issue. Otherwise, route to Support + the customer’s own monitoring.

Happy to help craft copy that makes this explicit without sounding evasive.

Kira
Enterprise Comms Guidance (Dedicated/Private)


---

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 10:05 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Security/compliance angle:
- Avoid any Console experience that implicitly confirms “customer X has Dedicated in region Y” to an unauth’d viewer or by shareable links.
- Any Dedicated/Private incident view in Console needs to be behind auth and scoped to the org/project. No public URLs.
- In post-incident summaries for Dedicated/Private: do not include customer identifiers, contract terms, or exact capacity/allocations.

For v1, I’d also prefer:
- No free-text fields in Console that can be updated ad hoc by on-call without review (or we need an approval path). Links to existing incident artifacts (Statuspage for Hosted; internal incident record rendered in Console for tenant-specific) are safer.

If we do render a Dedicated/Private “incident details” page, we should use the standardized templates (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/etc.) and a fixed set of fields.

Naomi
Security & Compliance


---

From: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 10:48 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Adding legal guardrails for any customer-facing incident/summaries surfaced in Console:
- Don’t characterize an event as an “SLA breach,” “violation,” or “guarantee,” and avoid promising credits/comp.
- Avoid definitive root cause statements until confirmed (use “we believe” / “preliminary” language as needed).
- Don’t include sensitive implementation details that materially increase security risk (e.g., exact dependency chain, vendor-specific misconfig details, exploit-like descriptions).

I like Kira’s split: public-only where truly shared, tenant-specific only behind auth.

If we need a single sentence to explain why a Dedicated incident isn’t on the public page, suggest something like:
“Some Dedicated/Private events can be specific to a customer environment and are communicated directly in Console and through Support.”

Michael Grant
Legal (Product)


---

From: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 11:26 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

From the implementation side:
- For Hosted, Statuspage gives us canonical incident URL + postmortem/summary URL once published. Console can just link out.
- For Dedicated/Private, we already capture incident state + timestamps + affected regions/components in incident-bot. We can expose a scoped “customer incident feed” endpoint for Console if we want.

One nuance: we need a hard rule for when Console shows a banner.
- If we only rely on manual tagging, we’ll miss cases.
- If we auto-trigger from alerts, we risk false positives.

My suggestion for v1: show Console banner only when IC/Comms Lead sets “customer_visible=true” (or equivalent) in incident-bot intake.

Also: for tenant-specific incidents, we should not reuse the public “post-incident summary” format verbatim unless we have a review step; but we can reuse the same headings.

Christine
Status Transparency (SRE)


---

From: Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 1:14 PM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

This helps.

Proposal for how we implement without inventing a whole new incident surface area:
- Hosted:
  - Console “Status” page shows a “Hosted services” section.
  - Pull incident list from Statuspage API and render titles + state + link out.
- Dedicated/Private:
  - Console “Status” page shows an “Your deployments” section.
  - Only show entries if (a) a tenant-scoped incident exists and (b) customer_visible flag is set.
  - Keep the details page minimal: phase (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved), affected regions, start time, and the templated update text.

We can add a small info line:
“Hosted incidents are posted publicly. Dedicated/Private events may be shared here when specific to your environment.”

Question for Marissa/Paula: are we comfortable with the “Status” nav item for v1, or should this ship as a banner + a link in the Help menu first?

Nora
Console (UX/Backend)


---

From: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 2:03 PM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Prefer a dedicated “Status” page for discoverability (Support has been pushing for this), but I’m ok scoping v1 to:
- Status nav item + basic list
- Banner only on incident-active state

Copy constraints (tying back to templates):
- We should re-use the standard status update blocks in Console as-is (Investigating/Identified/etc.) to avoid tone drift.
- No mention of “capacity shortfall” or “noisy neighbor” or “GPU allocation” in Dedicated updates.

Next step: Marissa can you confirm a decision so Design can finalize DES-244? Also: who is approving the Dedicated/Private in-Console copy — Support lead or Legal only for Sev0/1?

Paula
Product (Templates + Rollout)


---

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 4:42 PM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Decision for v1 (so we can close DES-244 scope and unblock build):

1) Hosted
- Console links out to the public status page incident + summary URLs.
- Console renders a lightweight list (no new copy beyond incident title/state + “View details”).

2) Dedicated
- Public status page only for shared Dedicated control-plane events (taxonomy will define those components).
- Tenant-specific Dedicated incidents: show only in Console behind auth, only when IC/Comms Lead sets customer_visible=true.
- Do not create public/shareable URLs.

3) Private
- No public incidents.
- Console may show tenant-scoped incidents only when explicitly flagged customer_visible=true and only for Redwood-managed control plane issues; otherwise the banner routes to Support.

Approval workflow (v1):
- Use the standardized templates.
- For Sev0/Sev1 tenant-scoped updates: Comms Lead drafts, IC approves; Support lead is optional if we’re changing customer-facing impact characterization. Legal only if we’re publishing a post-incident summary that references a security issue.

Nora — go ahead with the “Status” nav item.
Paula — please update PM-612 notes with the Dedicated/Private split + the one-liner explanation language (Michael’s suggestion is good).
Christine — can you confirm what fields we need incident-bot to emit for the customer-visible feed (state, timestamps, affected regions/components, update text)?

Thanks all.

Marissa

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>

What I’m trying to lock down today:
1) What we can show/link in Console for Hosted API incidents (public status page) vs Dedicated vs Private.
2) Any constraints for incident visibility (don’t leak customer-specific capacity issues, don’t imply SLA breach, etc.).
3) A minimum v1 scope that we can ship with the component taxonomy rollout.

Proposed UX (v1):
- Add a “Status” entry in the Console nav.
- If there is an active incident affecting the customer’s selected project/region, show a banner on relevant pages + a “View incident details” link.
- “Status” page lists:
  - Current component health (from public status where applicable)
  - Recent incidents (last 30 days) with links to the incident page
  - Post-incident summaries (same list; shown when available)

Open question (big one): Dedicated/Private.
- Hosted: straightforward (public status page + public summaries).
- Dedicated: we need guidance on whether we (a) link to public incident objects, (b) show something tenant-specific in Console, or (c) keep Dedicated as a generic “Control Plane / Provisioning” component only.
- Private: my assumption is no public status page. Do we show anything in Console beyond a generic “contact support” if we know the Private deployment is impacted?

Can folks reply with constraints + recommendation by EOD? I want to feed a crisp decision into DES-244/PM-612 and keep Nora unblocked on implementation.

Marissa
Product, Console & Rollouts


---

From: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 9:37 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

+1 on Hosted being easy.

Dedicated/Private constraints (current policy direction from PM-612 discussions):
- Dedicated incidents are not always “public,” because the blast radius can be one customer or a small set of customers. We should not post customer-specific Dedicated capacity constraints on the public status page.
- Private: same, but stricter. Many Private deployments are in customer-controlled environments; posting statuses publicly creates confusion and can disclose that a particular customer is running us.

Recommendation (v1):
- Hosted: link directly to status.redwoodinference.com incident + post-incident summary.
- Dedicated:
  - Public status page only for Dedicated *control plane* (provisioning, console auth to dedicated mgmt, etc.) when it is truly shared.
  - Customer-specific Dedicated data plane issues should show in Console only for the impacted org (banner + short text), and should not link to a public incident.
- Private:
  - No public incidents.
  - Console can show a generic banner only when we are confident it’s a Redwood-managed control plane issue. Otherwise, route to Support + the customer’s own monitoring.

Happy to help craft copy that makes this explicit without sounding evasive.

Kira
Enterprise Comms Guidance (Dedicated/Private)


---

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 10:05 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Security/compliance angle:
- Avoid any Console experience that implicitly confirms “customer X has Dedicated in region Y” to an unauth’d viewer or by shareable links.
- Any Dedicated/Private incident view in Console needs to be behind auth and scoped to the org/project. No public URLs.
- In post-incident summaries for Dedicated/Private: do not include customer identifiers, contract terms, or exact capacity/allocations.

For v1, I’d also prefer:
- No free-text fields in Console that can be updated ad hoc by on-call without review (or we need an approval path). Links to existing incident artifacts (Statuspage for Hosted; internal incident record rendered in Console for tenant-specific) are safer.

If we do render a Dedicated/Private “incident details” page, we should use the standardized templates (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/etc.) and a fixed set of fields.

Naomi
Security & Compliance


---

From: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 10:48 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Adding legal guardrails for any customer-facing incident/summaries surfaced in Console:
- Don’t characterize an event as an “SLA breach,” “violation,” or “guarantee,” and avoid promising credits/comp.
- Avoid definitive root cause statements until confirmed (use “we believe” / “preliminary” language as needed).
- Don’t include sensitive implementation details that materially increase security risk (e.g., exact dependency chain, vendor-specific misconfig details, exploit-like descriptions).

I like Kira’s split: public-only where truly shared, tenant-specific only behind auth.

If we need a single sentence to explain why a Dedicated incident isn’t on the public page, suggest something like:
“Some Dedicated/Private events can be specific to a customer environment and are communicated directly in Console and through Support.”

Michael Grant
Legal (Product)


---

From: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 11:26 AM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

From the implementation side:
- For Hosted, Statuspage gives us canonical incident URL + postmortem/summary URL once published. Console can just link out.
- For Dedicated/Private, we already capture incident state + timestamps + affected regions/components in incident-bot. We can expose a scoped “customer incident feed” endpoint for Console if we want.

One nuance: we need a hard rule for when Console shows a banner.
- If we only rely on manual tagging, we’ll miss cases.
- If we auto-trigger from alerts, we risk false positives.

My suggestion for v1: show Console banner only when IC/Comms Lead sets “customer_visible=true” (or equivalent) in incident-bot intake.

Also: for tenant-specific incidents, we should not reuse the public “post-incident summary” format verbatim unless we have a review step; but we can reuse the same headings.

Christine
Status Transparency (SRE)


---

From: Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 1:14 PM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

This helps.

Proposal for how we implement without inventing a whole new incident surface area:
- Hosted:
  - Console “Status” page shows a “Hosted services” section.
  - Pull incident list from Statuspage API and render titles + state + link out.
- Dedicated/Private:
  - Console “Status” page shows an “Your deployments” section.
  - Only show entries if (a) a tenant-scoped incident exists and (b) customer_visible flag is set.
  - Keep the details page minimal: phase (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved), affected regions, start time, and the templated update text.

We can add a small info line:
“Hosted incidents are posted publicly. Dedicated/Private events may be shared here when specific to your environment.”

Question for Marissa/Paula: are we comfortable with the “Status” nav item for v1, or should this ship as a banner + a link in the Help menu first?

Nora
Console (UX/Backend)


---

From: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 2:03 PM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Prefer a dedicated “Status” page for discoverability (Support has been pushing for this), but I’m ok scoping v1 to:
- Status nav item + basic list
- Banner only on incident-active state

Copy constraints (tying back to templates):
- We should re-use the standard status update blocks in Console as-is (Investigating/Identified/etc.) to avoid tone drift.
- No mention of “capacity shortfall” or “noisy neighbor” or “GPU allocation” in Dedicated updates.

Next step: Marissa can you confirm a decision so Design can finalize DES-244? Also: who is approving the Dedicated/Private in-Console copy — Support lead or Legal only for Sev0/1?

Paula
Product (Templates + Rollout)


---

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Nora Ahmed <nora.ahmed@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Kira Thompson <kira.thompson@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 15, 2025 at 4:42 PM
Subject: Re: Console linking to status incidents + post-incident summaries (Hosted vs Dedicated/Private constraints)

Decision for v1 (so we can close DES-244 scope and unblock build):

1) Hosted
- Console links out to the public status page incident + summary URLs.
- Console renders a lightweight list (no new copy beyond incident title/state + “View details”).

2) Dedicated
- Public status page only for shared Dedicated control-plane events (taxonomy will define those components).
- Tenant-specific Dedicated incidents: show only in Console behind auth, only when IC/Comms Lead sets customer_visible=true.
- Do not create public/shareable URLs.

3) Private
- No public incidents.
- Console may show tenant-scoped incidents only when explicitly flagged customer_visible=true and only for Redwood-managed control plane issues; otherwise the banner routes to Support.

Approval workflow (v1):
- Use the standardized templates.
- For Sev0/Sev1 tenant-scoped updates: Comms Lead drafts, IC approves; Support lead is optional if we’re changing customer-facing impact characterization. Legal only if we’re publishing a post-incident summary that references a security issue.

Nora — go ahead with the “Status” nav item.
Paula — please update PM-612 notes with the Dedicated/Private split + the one-liner explanation language (Michael’s suggestion is good).
Christine — can you confirm what fields we need incident-bot to emit for the customer-visible feed (state, timestamps, affected regions/components, update text)?

Thanks all.

Marissa

source.subject: Dedicated capacity: throughput guarantees and measurement methodology
messages:
From: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@acme.ai
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Hi Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We're mapping out requirements for a Dedicated GPU pool and need to pin expected throughput guarantees before we can move to procurement. A few specifics from our side:

- Workload: conversational assistant serving ~2000 concurrent users, typical reply length ~64 tokens, heavy peaks (burst) where concurrent requests can hit ~5k for a few minutes.
- Models under consideration: open-7b / opt-6.7b equivalent.
- Target SLOs: p95 latency under 300ms (when possible) for typical short replies; need a clear tokens/sec and requests/sec guarantee for committed capacity.

Questions:
1) How do you express throughput guarantees for Dedicated? Do you guarantee tokens/sec across the pool, requests/sec, or both?
2) What measurement methodology do you use to validate those guarantees (payloads, batching, seq length, warm vs cold cache)?
3) How do guarantees change with reserved commits vs on-demand dedicated capacity?

Procurement also asked about acceptable SLAs and whether we can get a price reduction tied to a 12-month commit. Can you loop in the right folks and share a proposal we can circulate internally?

Jordan
Jordan Patel
Head of Platform Engineering, Acme AI
procurement@acme.ai

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 13:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Hi Jordan — thanks for the clear outline. Looping Marissa (our account lead) and Vivek (product/serving runtime) to cover capacity sizing and SLOs. Below is an initial, pragmatic answer so you have something to share with procurement while we prepare a formal proposal PDF and test artifacts.

Short answer: we provide both tokens/sec (pool-level) and requests/sec (per-route, scaled by concurrency) guarantees for Dedicated when customers purchase a reserved commit. Guarantees are tied to a specific model family and a measurement profile (see details).

High-level guarantees (example for a 12x A100-equivalent pool running open-7b):
- Pool throughput: 120,000 tokens/sec (sustained, measured with steady-state synthetic traffic at 512-token seq, batching enabled).
- Requests/sec: 200 requests/sec for average 64-token responses with p95 latency <= 300ms.
- Burst behavior: buffer for short spikes up to 2x baseline for up to 3 minutes using reserved burst credit, beyond which automatic overflow to lower-cost fallback model/region occurs unless configured otherwise.

Measurement methodology (brief):
1) Benchmark harness: we run an isolated measurement harness that drives requests against the Dedicated pool from a controlled client VM inside the same region.
2) Profiles: tests use three profiles — short (16-64 tokens), medium (128-256), long (512 tokens). Guarantees are specified per-profile.
3) Batching: continuous batching is enabled by default for Dedicated; metrics are captured both with and without batching to show variance.
4) Warm state: we measure both cold-starts (first token) and warmed (after KV cache is populated); guarantees apply to sustained warmed-state performance.
5) Metrics & SLO: tokens/sec and requests/sec are measured as aggregate rates over 60s windows; latency targets are p95 and p99 snapshots over 5-minute windows during test runs.

How guarantees change with reserved commits vs on-demand:
- Reserved commit (monthly/annual): we allocate and reserve the GPU capacity exclusively; guarantees above apply with the committed pool. Pricing includes a discount tier tied to the commit term and size.
- On-demand Dedicated: softer/conditional guarantees — we aim for the same throughput but cannot provide contractual SLAs without a commit; peak isolation is best-effort.

I'll attach a draft proposal and our raw throughput test CSV for the 12x pool run. Rafael

Attachments: RI_Dedicated_Proposal_v3.pdf; Throughput_Test_Results.csv

--
Rafael Mendes
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwood.com
From: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@acme.ai
Date: Wed, 5 Nov 2025 10:41:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Thanks Rafael — this is helpful. A few follow ups from procurement/infra after reviewing the draft:

- Can you clarify the baseline assumptions behind the 120k tokens/sec number (model, batch size, sequence length distribution)?
- For billing/commit sizing, we need an estimate of sustained RPS for our normal profile (median reply ~64 tokens) and the expected cost delta if we reserve for 12 months vs 3 months.
- On bursts: we need explicit wording on how burst credits are replenished and whether those bursts count against the committed utilization (we must avoid hidden overage surcharges).

Also — small ask — procurement wants to see a third-party-friendly measurement method we can run ourselves (open harness) so we can validate the numbers during a POC. Do you provide a test harness or a runbook?

Jordan

On Mon, Nov 3, 2025 at 01:02 PM Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Jordan — thanks for the clear outline. Looping Marissa (our account lead) and Vivek (product/serving runtime) to cover capacity sizing and SLOs. Below is an initial, pragmatic answer so you have something to share with procurement while we prepare a formal proposal PDF and test artifacts.

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 7 Nov 2025 08:26:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Jordan — apologies for the slight delay, wanted to align pricing + the measurement/runbook before sending across. Below is a concise proposal and how we recommend you validate throughput during the POC. If you want, we can run the validation together and redact any sensitive telemetry.

What the numbers mean (baseline assumptions):
- Model: open-7b (float16 quantized profile we used in tests).
- Sequence-length distribution for the 120k tokens/sec figure: 60% short (<=64 tokens), 30% medium (65-256), 10% long (257-512). The benchmark that produced 120k tokens/sec used an average batch aggregation window of 30ms and a per-GPU batching target equivalent to a combined effective batch of 48 tokens.
- The 200 RPS estimate for 64-token replies assumes continuous batching and KV-cache warm. Without batching the same pool would sustain ~40-60 RPS.

Pricing & commit tiers (summary):
- 3-month reserved commit (12x A100-equiv pool): list price $55k/mo, 5% discount if paid annually upfront.
- 12-month reserved commit (12x A100-equiv pool): list price $50k/mo, standard commit discount 15% (applied to committed spend). Additional volume discounts available >24 months or >2x pool size.
- Overage policy: burst usage consumed from reserved burst credits for the committed pool. Credits replenish monthly at a rate equal to 10% of committed capacity; unconsumed credits do not roll over beyond the term. Overage beyond credits billed at on-demand dedicated rate (we can cap or cap+notify per contract).

Measurement runbook (what you can run):
1) We provide a lightweight open harness (script + telemetry collector) that you run in your VPC. It creates a controlled client that mimics your seq-length distribution and emits traffic with configurable concurrency, payloads, and routing (single-route or multi-route).
2) Test profiles: short/medium/long as Rafael described. Run each profile for 15 minutes steady-state (after a 2-minute warm-up).
3) Measurement windows: capture tokens/sec and RPS in 60s windows; collect p50/p95/p99 latencies.
4) Validation: your team can run the harness during the POC; we also supply our raw CSV of a prior run (attached) and a test checklist.

Attached: RI_Dedicated_Proposal_v3.pdf (pricing/terms) and Throughput_Test_Results.csv (raw run used to compute the example guarantees).

If procurement is comfortable with a 12-month commit we can propose the 15% discount and include SLA terms that (a) commit to pool-level tokens/sec and requests/sec as specified for the model profile, (b) include an availability SLA and credits for missed SLOs, and (c) a joint test plan during onboarding.

Next steps:
- If you confirm interest in the 12-month commit, I will share a formal MSA addendum + DocuSign for the reserved capacity.
- Ping me dates for a joint POC run and we’ll schedule Rafael & Vivek to assist.

Best,
Marissa Cole
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

From: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@acme.ai
Date: Mon, 10 Nov 2025 16:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Marissa — this all looks good and answers the procurement checklist. Two quick asks before we sign:

1) Can you add a clause to the MSA addendum that a joint POC measurement (we run the harness, Redwood verifies) will be the baseline for the initial month’s acceptance testing?
2) We request a one-time onboarding credit equal to one week of committed capacity during the first month (to help with ramping tooling and any calibration).

If you can include both, procurement will approve and we’ll return a signed DocuSign within 48 hours. Please attach the DocuSign link and I’ll get procurement to route.

Jordan

> On Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 08:26 AM Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Jordan — apologies for the slight delay, wanted to align pricing + the measurement/runbook before sending across.

Overage beyond credits billed at on-demand dedicated rate (we can cap or cap+notify per contract).

Measurement runbook (what you can run):
1) We provide a lightweight open harness (script + telemetry collector) that you run in your VPC. It creates a controlled client that mimics your seq-length distribution and emits traffic with configurable concurrency, payloads, and routing (single-route or multi-route).
2) Test profiles: short/medium/long as Rafael described. Run each profile for 15 minutes steady-state (after a 2-minute warm-up).
3) Measurement windows: capture tokens/sec and RPS in 60s windows; collect p50/p95/p99 latencies.
4) Validation: your team can run the harness during the POC; we also supply our raw CSV of a prior run (attached) and a test checklist.

Attached: RI_Dedicated_Proposal_v3.pdf (pricing/terms) and Throughput_Test_Results.csv (raw run used to compute the example guarantees).

If procurement is comfortable with a 12-month commit we can propose the 15% discount and include SLA terms that (a) commit to pool-level tokens/sec and requests/sec as specified for the model profile, (b) include an availability SLA and credits for missed SLOs, and (c) a joint test plan during onboarding.

Next steps:
- If you confirm interest in the 12-month commit, I will share a formal MSA addendum + DocuSign for the reserved capacity.
- Ping me dates for a joint POC run and we’ll schedule Rafael & Vivek to assist.

Best,
Marissa Cole
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

2) We request a one-time onboarding credit equal to one week of committed capacity during the first month (to help with ramping tooling and any calibration).

text:
From: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@acme.ai
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Hi Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We're mapping out requirements for a Dedicated GPU pool and need to pin expected throughput guarantees before we can move to procurement. A few specifics from our side:

- Workload: conversational assistant serving ~2000 concurrent users, typical reply length ~64 tokens, heavy peaks (burst) where concurrent requests can hit ~5k for a few minutes.
- Models under consideration: open-7b / opt-6.7b equivalent.
- Target SLOs: p95 latency under 300ms (when possible) for typical short replies; need a clear tokens/sec and requests/sec guarantee for committed capacity.

Questions:
1) How do you express throughput guarantees for Dedicated? Do you guarantee tokens/sec across the pool, requests/sec, or both?
2) What measurement methodology do you use to validate those guarantees (payloads, batching, seq length, warm vs cold cache)?
3) How do guarantees change with reserved commits vs on-demand dedicated capacity?

Procurement also asked about acceptable SLAs and whether we can get a price reduction tied to a 12-month commit. Can you loop in the right folks and share a proposal we can circulate internally?

Jordan
Jordan Patel
Head of Platform Engineering, Acme AI
procurement@acme.ai



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 13:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Hi Jordan — thanks for the clear outline. Looping Marissa (our account lead) and Vivek (product/serving runtime) to cover capacity sizing and SLOs. Below is an initial, pragmatic answer so you have something to share with procurement while we prepare a formal proposal PDF and test artifacts.

Short answer: we provide both tokens/sec (pool-level) and requests/sec (per-route, scaled by concurrency) guarantees for Dedicated when customers purchase a reserved commit. Guarantees are tied to a specific model family and a measurement profile (see details).

High-level guarantees (example for a 12x A100-equivalent pool running open-7b):
- Pool throughput: 120,000 tokens/sec (sustained, measured with steady-state synthetic traffic at 512-token seq, batching enabled).
- Requests/sec: 200 requests/sec for average 64-token responses with p95 latency <= 300ms.
- Burst behavior: buffer for short spikes up to 2x baseline for up to 3 minutes using reserved burst credit, beyond which automatic overflow to lower-cost fallback model/region occurs unless configured otherwise.

Measurement methodology (brief):
1) Benchmark harness: we run an isolated measurement harness that drives requests against the Dedicated pool from a controlled client VM inside the same region.
2) Profiles: tests use three profiles — short (16-64 tokens), medium (128-256), long (512 tokens). Guarantees are specified per-profile.
3) Batching: continuous batching is enabled by default for Dedicated; metrics are captured both with and without batching to show variance.
4) Warm state: we measure both cold-starts (first token) and warmed (after KV cache is populated); guarantees apply to sustained warmed-state performance.
5) Metrics & SLO: tokens/sec and requests/sec are measured as aggregate rates over 60s windows; latency targets are p95 and p99 snapshots over 5-minute windows during test runs.

How guarantees change with reserved commits vs on-demand:
- Reserved commit (monthly/annual): we allocate and reserve the GPU capacity exclusively; guarantees above apply with the committed pool. Pricing includes a discount tier tied to the commit term and size.
- On-demand Dedicated: softer/conditional guarantees — we aim for the same throughput but cannot provide contractual SLAs without a commit; peak isolation is best-effort.

I'll attach a draft proposal and our raw throughput test CSV for the 12x pool run. Rafael

Attachments: RI_Dedicated_Proposal_v3.pdf; Throughput_Test_Results.csv

--
Rafael Mendes
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwood.com


From: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@acme.ai
Date: Wed, 5 Nov 2025 10:41:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Thanks Rafael — this is helpful. A few follow ups from procurement/infra after reviewing the draft:

- Can you clarify the baseline assumptions behind the 120k tokens/sec number (model, batch size, sequence length distribution)?
- For billing/commit sizing, we need an estimate of sustained RPS for our normal profile (median reply ~64 tokens) and the expected cost delta if we reserve for 12 months vs 3 months.
- On bursts: we need explicit wording on how burst credits are replenished and whether those bursts count against the committed utilization (we must avoid hidden overage surcharges).

Also — small ask — procurement wants to see a third-party-friendly measurement method we can run ourselves (open harness) so we can validate the numbers during a POC. Do you provide a test harness or a runbook?

Jordan

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Patel <jordan.patel@acme.ai>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 7 Nov 2025 08:26:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — throughput & SLA questions

Jordan — apologies for the slight delay, wanted to align pricing + the measurement/runbook before sending across. Below is a concise proposal and how we recommend you validate throughput during the POC. If you want, we can run the validation together and redact any sensitive telemetry.

What the numbers mean (baseline assumptions):
- Model: open-7b (float16 quantized profile we used in tests).
- Sequence-length distribution for the 120k tokens/sec figure: 60% short (<=64 tokens), 30% medium (65-256), 10% long (257-512). The benchmark that produced 120k tokens/sec used an average batch aggregation window of 30ms and a per-GPU batching target equivalent to a combined effective batch of 48 tokens.
- The 200 RPS estimate for 64-token replies assumes continuous batching and KV-cache warm. Without batching the same pool would sustain ~40-60 RPS.

Pricing & commit tiers (summary):
- 3-month reserved commit (12x A100-equiv pool): list price $55k/mo, 5% discount if paid annually upfront.
- 12-month reserved commit (12x A100-equiv pool): list price $50k/mo, standard commit discount 15% (applied to committed spend). Additional volume discounts available >24 months or >2x pool size.
- Overage policy: burst usage consumed from reserved burst credits for the committed pool. Credits replenish monthly at a rate equal to 10% of committed capacity; unconsumed credits do not roll over beyond the term. Overage beyond credits billed at on-demand dedicated rate (we can cap or cap+notify per contract).

Measurement runbook (what you can run):
1) We provide a lightweight open harness (script + telemetry collector) that you run in your VPC. It creates a controlled client that mimics your seq-length distribution and emits traffic with configurable concurrency, payloads, and routing (single-route or multi-route).
2) Test profiles: short/medium/long as Rafael described. Run each profile for 15 minutes steady-state (after a 2-minute warm-up).
3) Measurement windows: capture tokens/sec and RPS in 60s windows; collect p50/p95/p99 latencies.
4) Validation: your team can run the harness during the POC; we also supply our raw CSV of a prior run (attached) and a test checklist.

Attached: RI_Dedicated_Proposal_v3.pdf (pricing/terms) and Throughput_Test_Results.csv (raw run used to compute the example guarantees).

If procurement is comfortable with a 12-month commit we can propose the 15% discount and include SLA terms that (a) commit to pool-level tokens/sec and requests/sec as specified for the model profile, (b) include an availability SLA and credits for missed SLOs, and (c) a joint test plan during onboarding.

Next steps:
- If you confirm interest in the 12-month commit, I will share a formal MSA addendum + DocuSign for the reserved capacity.
- Ping me dates for a joint POC run and we’ll schedule Rafael & Vivek to assist.

Best,

source.subject: Status page transparency upgrades — scope approval + measurement plan ask
source.body:
From: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Status page transparency upgrades — scope approval + measurement plan ask

Neha — sharing the updated brief for the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative for approval.

What changed since last review:
- Component taxonomy now explicitly splits by (product surface x region x capability) where it helps customers (e.g., Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings, by US-East/US-West/EU-West).
- Automation approach is “assistive by default”: incident-bot drafts updates from incident state + metrics, Comms Lead/IC owns final publish.
- Added guardrails on language: no implied SLA/credits; avoid customer-specific capacity discussion; Dedicated/Private incidents handled with product-appropriate visibility.

Decisions we’re seeking from you:
1) Approve the taxonomy direction (granularity + naming)
2) Approve the automation scope (incident-bot drafting + statuspage API client, not fully autonomous state flips)
3) Confirm measurement expectations for week 7–8 (TTFU, accuracy audit, support deflection)

Attachments:
- StatusPage_Transparency_Upgrades_Project_Brief_v1.2.pdf
- Statuspage_Component_Taxonomy_v0.9.docx

If you’re aligned, we’ll start Week 1–2 design (taxonomy + signal mapping) immediately and keep you posted via a weekly rollout note.

Thanks,
Talia Benson
Program Manager, Reliability Initiatives
Redwood Inference


---

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 10:01 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page transparency upgrades — scope approval + measurement plan ask

Approved on (1) and (2).

A few notes / constraints to hold the line on:
- We need more precision without turning the status page into an internal services diagram. Product-surface + region is the right customer mental model. Keep “capability” splits only where it’s a real, recurring failure mode (Streaming is a good example).
- Do not auto-publish component status changes off metrics alone in this phase. Drafts + prompts + validation steps are the correct direction.
- Dedicated / Private: we can’t imply anything that creates a contractual interpretation. Messaging should stay consistent with our public language and avoid customer-specific capacity/placement references.

Measurement plan: I want an explicit plan attached to the brief by end of week.
Please include:
- TTFU target (time-to-first-update) by severity (e.g., Sev0/Sev1) and how incident-bot reminders enforce it
- Accuracy audit method (how we detect mismatches between impacted routes/regions vs what we marked on the status page)
- Support impact: how we measure ticket deflection / reduction in “status page inaccurate” escalations
- Rollout gates: dogfood → limited public → full rollout, with rollback criteria

Also, please identify a single DRI for the component mapping source-of-truth (repo/config) and a review/approval path. I don’t want this drifting across Confluence + spreadsheets.

Thanks,
Neha Kapoor
VP, Infrastructure & Reliability
Redwood Inference


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 10:18 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page transparency upgrades — scope approval + measurement plan ask

+1 to all of Neha’s points.

On mapping source-of-truth: SRE recommends we keep the component registry + mapping config in-repo with schema validation (PR review required) and treat Confluence as the human-readable reference. Rafael can own the operational process; Christine (implementer) will do the first pass on registry entries with runtime/observability review.

On accuracy audits: we can use the weekly job we discussed to compare incident tags (component_id/region/capability) against status updates and flag mismatches for follow-up.

Sean


---

From: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 11:27 AM PT
Subject: Re: Status page transparency upgrades — scope approval + measurement plan ask

Thanks — we’ll incorporate the measurement plan into the brief and circulate by EOW.

Draft measurement plan (for alignment now):

1) TTFU targets (public status update)
- Sev0: first public update within 15 minutes of declaration (target), reminder at +10 min via incident-bot; escalation ping to IC + Comms Lead at +15.
- Sev1: first public update within 30 minutes (target), reminders at +20/+30.
- Metric source: incident-bot timestamps (incident declared vs first Statuspage publish event).

2) Accuracy audits
- Weekly automated audit job: incident tags (affected component_id/region/capability + impacted routes) compared to what was marked on Statuspage.
- Outputs: (a) mismatch count, (b) mismatch types (wrong region, too coarse component, missing capability split), (c) time-to-correct status, (d) owners for follow-up.
- We’ll sample 100% of Sev0/Sev1 and 20% of Sev2 for the first month.

3) Support impact / deflection
- Baseline: count of tickets with “status inaccurate” / “status page says OK but X failing” (Support will provide tags/macros).
- KPI: 30% reduction in those escalations by end of Week 8 (directional; not a hard SLA), plus time-to-resolution for disputes.
- Qualitative: Support survey after 2 weeks of rollout (“fewer back-and-forth” / “easier to point customers at component-level incident and summary”).

4) Rollout gates
- Dogfood (internal view + incident drills): validate taxonomy + bot workflow + anti-flap logic.
- Limited public (subset of components/regions): monitor for confusion + flapping + excessive comms overhead.
- Full rollout: only after we meet (a) TTFU adherence >= 80% for Sev0/Sev1 in dogfood + limited public and (b) mismatch rate trending down week-over-week.
- Rollback criteria: repeated component flapping; significant customer confusion; on-call load increase without benefit.

DRI for mapping source-of-truth: proposing Ravi Menon (service-to-component mapping) for the mapping layer, with Rafael as approval owner (SRE) and Allison for observability schema/labels review. We’ll route PR reviews through SRE + Observability for changes.

If you want different DRIs, tell us and we’ll adjust.

Talia


---

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 12:03 PM PT
Subject: Re: Status page transparency upgrades — scope approval + measurement plan ask

This works.

Two tweaks:
- Please phrase the support KPI as “goal / expected outcome” rather than a commitment (agree with your note).
- For rollout gates, add a specific on-call burden check: “median time spent on comms tasks” for IC/Comms Lead, pre vs post.

DRIs proposed are fine. Ensure changes to mapping have a clear review checklist (naming, region coverage, dashboards/alerts links, and Dedicated/Private sensitivity).

Neha
